## Cases mentioned in passing

### C-1 / Sea Wolf submarine welding failure
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "C-1 / Sea Wolf submarine welding failure"
- **Anchor:** `§2.p5`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "One of the first people I met in Washington was Millard Firebaugh. Millard was a captain at the time, head of the SSN-21 program — the Sea Wolf submarine, in the 1980s when it got cancelled by Congress. Then he had to go up to the Hill, they got it reinstated, and then when the Sea Wolf had a problem — this is getting to the point where some of you were barely born — it ended up costing the Navy $2 billion. At that point Captain Firebaugh, who was a graduate of this program before my time, was then Admiral Firebaugh, Chief Engineer of the Navy, and he put me on the committee with a bunch of..."

### C-1 / Sea Wolf submarine welding failure
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "C-1 / Sea Wolf submarine welding failure"
- **Anchor:** `§9.p1`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "One of the points I'm trying to make — this is one of the two or three for today's lecture — is that we use metals because they have good toughness, and we don't use ceramics because they have lousy toughness. Their critical flaw size is so small that you can never inspect for those things. The Sea Wolf submarine problem, to get back to a Navy example, is that they ended up getting too high carbon content and alloy content. The weld metal was 30 or 40% higher strength than it was intended to be, because of the chemistry, and they got a less tough steel. It formed little microscopic flaws that..."
